1→---
     2→title: "Introduction - Production Management"
     3→part: 10
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2447-2456"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**PRODUCTION MANAGEMENT**
     9→
    10→**Role of Production Management in a corporate**  
    11→The main role of the production team comprises of transforming raw materials into finished product ensuring cost efficiency and maintaining ethical, quality and safety standards during the production process. Resource management is an important role of the team which will ensure availability of raw materials, labour and machinery on time. These raw materials need to undergo testing to ensure good quality materials are being used for the production.  
    12→   
    13→White and black storages in the manufacturing premises need to be identified and production and dispatch of manufactured goods to required destinations are to be monitored on a daily basis.
    14→
    15→Production management also needs to look into possible improvements in processes from time to time to optimise time and costs of production.   
    16→In a typical EPC project, the cost of products may constitute 60% to 80% of the total EPC cost. However, in a service oriented industry, the reverse may be true.
    17→

1→---
     2→title: "JIT AND INSPECTION ISSUES"
     3→part: 10
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2457-2488"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : JIT AND INSPECTION ISSUES**
     9→
    10→First let us discuss on the JIT (Just in Time) issues. Just in Time refers to receipt or dispatch of materials exactly at the time it is required.
    11→
    12→When raw materials are requisitioned for the manufacturing process, the timing and the quantity of such raw materials is of utmost importance. Often it is found that the Plant manager do not place orders for the raw materials on time. Therefore either the raw material arrives too early creating a unnecessary inventory or it arrives late which does not allow manufacturing to commence, in other words, its idling. Either way it is a waste of money for the organization. Cost of inventory is the interest lost on the value of materials stored in the inventory based on the time of the storage. Cost of idling is the amount of loss of manpower and machinery that would have been utilized had there been no idling.
    13→
    14→Also it has sometimes so happened that a combination of raw materials is required to manufacture a product but the right quantity of combination has not arrived. For example in the case of a product called Bolts & Nuts, there are three components; the bolt, the nut and the washer. All the three components need to arrive at the works in the right quantities.
    15→
    16→The Plant manager has to take into account the delivery schedules of the raw material manufacturers and the consumption pattern of the raw materials being consumed in the plant and place orders accordingly. 
    17→
    18→All the above that we discussed was for JIT of raw materials entering the manufacturing premises. Similarly, JIT for the product dispatched must also be considered wisely by the agency receiving the finished products. The agency could be another department of the organization itself or some other organization. 
    19→
    20→In case the finished product is received too early by the recipient, then the agency will incur cost of inventory and likewise if it arrives late it will incur cost of idling.
    21→
    22→Therefore, all the stakeholders, the raw material manufacturer, the product manufacturer and the agency receiving the product have to chalk out their timelines of requirements and each place an order on the other accordingly. Just like we know about Supply chain, this can be termed as a Manufacturing-Implementation chain. The timelines of this chain can be so planned that it can reduce or eliminate cost of inventory and/or cost of idling.
    23→
    24→Now let us address the inspection issues. For a contractor which has its own manufacturing unit, it has several stages of inspection. First stage would be when the raw materials ordered from other third party manufacturers enter the manufacturing premises. Second would be when the finished product is dispatched from the manufacturer works. And the third and last would be when the contractor itself or the agency received the finished products.
    25→
    26→The above inspections are sometimes not done properly at either stage. Remember, the inspection relates to both the quality and quantity of the materials. Checking quality could be limited to only physical inspection at the time of receipt of materials but the mechanical and chemical properties can be checked upon various tests in the laboratories/test stations that the materials or products are subjected to.
    27→
    28→Once it so happened that the Stores manager did not record the quantity of each of the different steel materials required for erecting several structures dispatched from a third party manufacturer. The quantity of the steel materials was huge and there was no meticulous approach by the Stores manager to record the different quantities received. However, the manufacturer obtained the signature of the Stores manager to have received all the materials in full quantities. Once the construction manager was about to finish erecting all the structures, it was observed that some of the steel materials required to erect the last 5 structures were missing. On reconciliation, it was observed that the manufacturer had supplied all the materials and the Stores manager had also signed it off. Thereafter, the organization had to place another order for the missing materials at additional cost probably for the mistake in recording quantities by the Stores manager. Not only this, the construction for the last 5 structures was stranded with its manpower and equipment idling at site till the missing materials were received at site. This cost the organization significantly which was obviously not considered in the bid.
    29→
    30→In another instance, galvanized steel structures were dispatched on scheduled time to the contractor but the contractor was going slowly in the project. The contractor did not modify the timelines of delivery of the structures and therefore these structured lay idle in the stores. Over a period of time, white rust formed on the galvanized structures and the client questioned on the quality of the galavnisation and eventually rejected the use of these materials in the construction. The organization had to supply these materials once again and lost lot of money for the same apart from further delayed construction.   
    31→
    32→**The salient points are:**
    33→
    34→1. Just in Time management must be exercised by each stakeholder, the raw material manufacturer, the product manufacturer and the implementing agency so that the cost of inventory and cost of idling can be minimized.  
    35→2. Inspection of raw materials at works on receipt, inspection of manufactured product at dispatch and inspection of product received by the implementing agency at stores or site must be carried out meticulously both in terms of quality and quantity.  
    36→3. Quality and quantity must be recorded in clear terms with signatures of the stakeholders concerned to avoid conflicts or misunderstanding between stakeholders at any point of time.
    37→

1→---
     2→title: "Some Good Practices"
     3→part: 10
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2489-2499"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→**Resource Management**  
    11→Non availability of materials, labour and machinery on time will lead to idling of the plant. 
    12→
    13→**Quality**  
    14→You cannot ensure quality of a finished product until the raw materials are themselves tested for good quality. Poor quality raw materials cannot make a good quality finished product even if the manufacturing process is state of the art. Also important is that the quality of raw materials arriving in different batches need to be consistent as per standards. 
    15→
    16→**Automated process**  
    17→As far as possible, manual process must be eliminated to ensure a good manufacturing process.
    18→

1→---
     2→title: "Introduction - Supply Chain Management"
     3→part: 11
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2502-2512"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**SUPPLY CHAIN MANAGEMENT**  
     9→	  
    10→**Role of Supply Chain Management in a corporate**  
    11→The main role comprises of overseeing the flow of goods and information, and the finances from supplier to customers. Receipt or dispatch of finished goods from the manufacturing plant and transportation of the same to the stores or site is the chain that this department refers to. If the organisation also has a raw material manufacturing plant, then it has also to be engaged in receipt or dispatch of raw materials from the plant and transportation of the same to the finished good manufacturing plant. 
    12→
    13→Preparation of enquiry to be sent to vendors, call for tenders for selection of vendors, negotiation and finalisation of vendors, orders to vendors, designs and testing for the raw materials and finished products to be supplied by the vendors, commencement of manufacturing, inspection of raw material and products, transportation of the finished products from the factories to the site stores, inspection of quantities arrived at stores, transportation of these products from the site stores to the site locations is what constitute the complete “chain” of this team. Therefore sourcing, logistics and distribution are the key roles of a Supply Chain management team. 
    14→
    15→Supply Chain also needs to look into risks involved in the chain like disruptions in supply and demand fluctuations so that they can be mitigated on time for a seamless business operation. Modern techniques for forecasting and inventory management is also a need for the team to consider.
    16→
    17→There are three broad components of a project – (1) Supply (2) Services and (3) Management to implement the Supplies and Services in place for completion of the project. Normally in an infrastructure project, Supplies may contribute to 60% to 80% of the contract value, the balance for the Services portion which will include approximately 5% to 10% for the Project Management. These percentages may vary from project to project but provides a fair idea of the weightages of the three components in an infrastructure project. Therefore SCM is greatly responsible for the competitive advantage that it provides to the organization.
    18→

1→---
     2→title: "NEGOTIATIONS IGNORING PARTNERSHIPS"
     3→part: 11
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2513-2532"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER :  NEGOTIATIONS IGNORING PARTNERSHIPS**
     9→
    10→In any business, a contractor or a manufacturer have multiple options to select from dozens of subcontractors and sub manufacturers for their production or services. In every business opportunity, if you try to find the least priced qualified vendor, you may do so and gain in the initial stages of your business to arrive at lowest priced bid, but eventually you shall find that you are considering different vendors for different business opportunities and therefore you will fail to build a trust and understanding with these vendors in the long run.
    11→
    12→Therefore it will be more appropriate to form a selection panel, lay out the criteria & terms and conditions for selection, and then negotiate and select a few, say three for an example out of dozens available, for a stipulated period of time like one year, to be designated as their Supply partners. Building such supplier partnerships helps in numerous ways; 
    13→
    14→1\. You don’t need to go through a selection process every time and waste time and energy for the same, rather concentrate who could be the supplier amongst the chosen three partners only  
    15→2\. Since these partners will be receiving the orders for various business opportunities, they will have the mass orders thus reducing the cost of products  
    16→3\. You may have a standard pricing of different items right at the beginning of such partnerships, subject to permissible  
    17→4\. Due to such mass orders, the partnerships will build trust amongst them and cooperate and support contractors in good and bad times.
    18→
    19→Some “so called intelligent” Supply Chain managers often ignore requirement of such partnerships and resort to selection of the least priced qualified vendor for every business opportunity. Eventually they find the process too cumbersome and not ineffective in the long run. These managers are keen to prove their success to their superiors but do not think about the organisation’s long term goals.
    20→
    21→**The salient points are:**
    22→
    23→1. Build supplier partnerships with validity for a stipulated period of time  
    24→2. Partnerships builds trust and cooperation to prepare effective costs  
    25→3. Partnerships do away with lengthy processes involved for every awards.
    26→

1→---
     2→title: "WIN-LOSE ATTITUDE"
     3→part: 11
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2533-2563"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : WIN-LOSE ATTITUDE**
     9→
    10→One of the important characteristic of selecting partners is to build trust. Therefore in a partnership agreement, the Manager must ensure that the terms are unbiased, vendor friendly and leads to a win-win situation making both the contractor/manufacturer and their subcontractors happy with good understanding between them.
    11→
    12→Since subcontractors or small manufacturers are craving for orders in order to sustain their organisation, some contractors resort to exploiting the vendors by stipulating biased and favourable terms for them in their partnership agreement. Again, initially you may profit out of such exploited terms in the short run, but such partners will slowly develop mistrust and opt out of partnerships in the long term or they may be in search of a different partner. This news may spread in the market and other good vendors may refrain to enter into partnerships with the organization in the future.
    13→
    14→An a example, stringent and favourable one-way terms biased towards the contractor vis-à-vis unbiased win-win terms are illustrated in the following table.
    15→
    16→| Terms | Biased approach | Unbiased approach |
    17→| :---- | :---- | :---- |
    18→| Validity of offer | 12 months | 6 months |
    19→| Liquidated Damages | 0.5% of total order value | 0.1% of balance work value |
    20→| Advance Payment | 5% of order value | 10% of order value |
    21→| Progressive Payment | 75% of executed value | 80% of executed value |
    22→| Retention Payment | 20% of order value | 10% of order value |
    23→| Warranty period | 18 months | 12 months |
    24→
    25→There could be lot of other specification criteria like the above ones. Remember fair relationship is about give and take. If you want to take the entire cake, it may work for a short while but in the long run it will surely backfire on you. Hence if an organization is serious in their partnerships to work, you must stipulate a fair, unbiased, standard and win-win terms for both the partners. In such an agreement, you may have advantage in some terms but disadvantage in some others based on the stature of your organization. 
    26→
    27→Just think of your friends. A true friend is said to be “A friend in need is a friend indeed”. At times you may require support of other friends, at other times, you may be required to provide support to your other friends. That’s how true friendship works. That’s how true relationship works. And that’s how unbiased win-win partnerships work. 
    28→
    29→Once it so happened that a contractor quoted for a big Rs 1000 Cr project. The bid was valid for 3 months. The supporting manufacturers provided 4 months offer validity to the contractor. Due to delayed evaluation by the client, the client requested all bidders to extend their bid validities by another 3 months. The project was very important to the contractor. But the aluminum prices raised so much during the past three months that the conductor manufacturer was reluctant to provide extended validity of their offer (their initial offer value was Rs 300 Cr) for another three months. In view of this, the contractor was not in a position to provide this extended validity. 
    30→
    31→The contractor and the supporting conductor manufacturer sat together. They were partners for long. While the conductor was very keen to win the tender, the conductor manufacturer was not so keen because of his envisaged reduced margins due to escalated costs of aluminum. However, in view of long partnerships which helped the manufacturer to execute voluminous orders received from contractor, the manufacturer finally agreed to extend the requested validity. Eventually the contractor was found to be the lease priced responsive bidder and was eventually awarded the project. Trust and cooperation of the manufacturer prevailed because of win-win partnerships in the past. In true win-win partnerships, a partner may come to your rescue at difficult times. That’s the true relationship that a partnership possess.  
    32→
    33→**The salient points are:**
    34→
    35→1. While you build partnership with other agencies, stipulate unbiased terms in the agreement to foster a win-win partnership  
    36→2. True partnership renders trust and cooperation amongst partners  
    37→3. Partners support each other in challenging and difficult times and enjoy good revenues and profits together at favourable times.
    38→

1→---
     2→title: "DELAYED ORDERS"
     3→part: 11
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2564-2577"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : DELAYED ORDERS**
     9→
    10→Vendors are unlikely to provide to bidders validity of their offers for long duration beyond the validity of the bid as stipulated by the bidder’s client. In fact you are lucky if you get validity of offers beyond the validity of the bid as stipulated by the client. Placement of order to a vendor by a contractor beyond the validity of his offer is often very risky. In such a case, depending on the market costs, the vendor may not agree to prices provided in their own offer taking advantage of the expiry of their offer validity and he has the right to do so officially or legally.
    11→
    12→In the event that the contractor has to accept escalated offers from the vendor after the expiry of their offer validity, then the estimated profit levels of the contractor will reduce right at the beginning of the project itself, not to speak of what will happen during the course of execution of the project where it could further reduce due to various adverse factors.
    13→
    14→It has happened so many times that the Supply Chain Manager have delayed placing orders to their vendors when the validity of the offers provided by the vendors have already expired. This is a very dangerous situation because any such vendor may exploit the situation or else he may really be not in a position to sustain the prices provided in their initial offers.  
    15→
    16→**The salient points are:**
    17→
    18→1. Once an organization becomes L1 in a bid, the SCM should commence their preparatory work in view of the anticipated award.  
    19→2. Bidders must ensure that they take one month more of validity of offers from their vendors than that stipulated by the client in the bid.  
    20→3. Bidders must place orders on their vendors within the above mentioned one month extra validity of the vendor in case the client places order on the contractor at the end of the validity period of the bid
    21→

1→---
     2→title: "THE BEST IS NOT REQUIRED ALWAYS"
     3→part: 11
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2578-2592"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : THE BEST IS NOT REQUIRED ALWAYS**
     9→
    10→How many toppers of the best institutions of the world have become Managing Directors and CEOs of big companies? I think it’s seldom. While excellent qualification does have importance for a good career, it’s not only the criteria to become the CXOs of organisations. There are plenty of other attributes required such as the leadership qualities, understanding of business, vision and potential to stategise new inventions to remain as the leader in the market, develop USPs, etc. etc. 
    11→
    12→When you purchase any appliance from the market, you are aware that there are quite a few good manufacturers from whom you may purchase. Out of them, one may indisputably the best in the industry, but that does not mean others are bad. The other manufacturers too can be very good if not as good as the best one. Therefore one can purchase the appliance from any of them.
    13→
    14→Similarly, for a vendor partnership, you may not necessarily select the best vendor as perceived in the market. Rather there are many more attributes to be considered. Like, apart from being the best or a good manufacturer, we need to look into their offers, terms and conditions being accepted by the vendor, capability to execute, their factory load, ethical and quality standards, etc. Often big reputed vendors have taken orders but have failed to execute an order because of order overbook which were actually beyond their capacity to execute.
    15→
    16→Looking into all the terms and conditions as accepted by a vendor and of course it’s reputation and past records, a vendor can be selected who may not be the best one by reputation but definitely good as per past records. 
    17→
    18→**The salient points are:**
    19→
    20→1. You may not necessarily select the best vendor always; at times you can always select any other good vendor  
    21→2. You cannot select a vendor based on its quality of the product or service only; you need to look into other parameters like its capacity, timelines to deliver, financials, acceptance of techno-commercial terms and conditions, ethical and technical standards, past experience records, customer satisfaction certificates, etc
    22→

1→---
     2→title: "MISDEEDS OF SCM MANAGERS"
     3→part: 11
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2593-2607"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : MISDEEDS OF SCM MANAGERS**
     9→
    10→**Receiving favours from vendors**  
    11→In many organistaions, money exchange hands for providing undue favours to vendors, one of them being on award to a vendor for a business opportunity.
    12→
    13→**Wrong Logistics**  
    14→Say in a widely scattered project, there are many stores established at different locations to support the work nearby the stores. Therefore each store has a particular requirement of materials that would be implemented in the works around the particular stores. Thus the SCM Head has to be very careful to transport the required materials at each store. If the logistics are not well organised, often the material required in one store goes to the other or the materials of a specified quantity goes to another store where the quantity required is less or more than what it receives. In case quantity of materials received is less, the SCM has to dispatch the balance quantity of materials either from the manufacturing plant or from any other store where materials have been received in excess. If the store has received excess materials, then this excess quantity has to transport to another store where they have shortage of materials.
    15→
    16→This re-transportation of materials not only incurs additional costs to the company but also delays the execution of the project. Therefore re-transportation and delay in receiving the right quantity of materials add to the losses for the project.
    17→
    18→**The salient points are:**
    19→
    20→1. A technical audit must take place to check if an order is being placed to the deserving supplier  
    21→2. Multi location logistics must be well planned before actual dispatches
    22→

1→---
     2→title: "Some Good Practices"
     3→part: 11
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2608-2624"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→**Build good partnerships with win-win attitude**  
    11→The key to win projects is to build reliable and trust worthy partnerships. The agreement between such partners must be fair, acceptable with a win-win situation for both the parties. Sometimes in the short run, an organization may ditch a regular partner and join hands with another for better gains, but this does not work out well in the long run. Remember, an organization is not built to do business for a year, it has a plan to run for decades. Therefore long term partnerships benefiting an organization in the long term is always desirable
    12→
    13→**Maintain partnerships even in unfavourable times**  
    14→Sometimes in adverse market situations, regular partners are unable to provide the benefits that it usually had. You may then say “good bye” to these partners and join hands with others. As I mentioned before, staying afloat with your partners in such difficult times proves beneficial in the long run.
    15→
    16→**Place orders on L1 status**  
    17→It takes time for a client to place an order to a contractor even after the announcement of price bids and some bidder being the L1 bidder. After the price opening, the bids will be evaluated for its responsiveness, correctness, seeking clarifications from the bidders, and thereafter finalizing the bidder for the order. So as and when a bid is deemed to be L1, the SCM department, in order to save precious time and place orders within the validity of the offers of the vendors, can proceed negotiating with vendors towards placement of orders. However, placement of orders to the vendors should be done only after the client has placed the order to the bidder.
    18→
    19→**Effective communication with suppliers**  
    20→Communication with suppliers on a day to day basis is very important. Any misunderstanding will lead to delayed deliveries and will hamper project execution.
    21→
    22→**Effective forecasting**  
    23→Incorrect forecasting will lead to stockouts or overstocking. Stockouts will render idling at site whereas overstocking will increase the inventory. In both cases, the project cost is escalated.
    24→

1→---
     2→title: "Introduction - Fleet & Equipment Management"
     3→part: 12
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2627-2643"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**FLEET AND EQUIPMENT MANAGEMENT**
     9→
    10→**Role of Fleet and Equipment management in a corporate**  
    11→Fleet Manager oversees the fleet of vehicles, operational efficiency and delivery of goods from place to place. It also oversees the lifecycle of vehicles, the mandatory licenses and paperwork, hiring and supervising drivers and optimization of transport routes. Regular maintenance and fuel consumption analysis is also done to optimise costs. Legal and safety standards of all vehicles also need to be ensured. Vehicle performance, fuel efficiency and driver behaviour are also monitored from time to time. 
    12→
    13→Equipment Manager is responsible for acquiring, deploying, maintaining, optimizing and retiring equipment. All equipment needs to comply with regulatory and safety standards.
    14→
    15→An organisation may have purchased or rented, or may further purchase or rent equipment and machinery to be provided to sites as and when required. Each such purchase has a capital cost and depreciation cost attached to it. Annual or monthly rentals also add to the overall costs.
    16→
    17→Managers must ensure that the returns against cost or rentals of equipment are optimised. In fact each and every equipment must record a return to cost parameter annually.  Just like if a property owner lets out his flat on rent, he must ensure that the annual rentals far exceeds the cost of maintenance of the flat including paying property/water tax. In other words, he makes money by letting out the flat. Similarly an equipment manager must ensure that he sets a profit target against usage of equipment provided to site. By this, he can justify the purchase or rent of the equipment by the organisation. These “internal profits” add to the overall profits of a company. By setting such profit targets, the manager can ensure that usage of equipment is optimised.
    18→
    19→In a similar manner, the Manager has to purchase or rent vehicles for transportation of materials from one place to the other. An organization may use the fleet of its vendors for transportation of goods from their manufacturing units to the stores of the organization. However, it may keep its own vehicles for regular transportation of materials from its stores to project locations. 
    20→
    21→Each vehicle purchased or rented also incurs costs. And therefore optimizing these costs is also very important. The utilisation of vehicles must be justified vis-à-vis the costs incurred. Just like if you buy a car but go for an outing with the car only for once in a year, then the cost of buying the car cannot be justified because going out once a year could well be done by hiring a cab. 
    22→
    23→The fleet of vehicles and the list of plant and machinery have to be recorded with information on as to when was it purchased or rented, the costs of purchase/rent, depreciation costs and maintenance costs, and the usage of the vehicles and equipment to determine the effectiveness of the purchases/rents.
    24→

1→---
     2→title: "USAGE NOT MONITORED"
     3→part: 12
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2644-2659"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : USAGE NOT MONITORED**
     9→
    10→We have already discussed on the optimum use of vehicle and equipment at site. This is often ignored. The FEM Manager has to discuss with the Project Manager and evaluate the productivity of work executed with each vehicle and equipment every month. In fact it can be treated as a mini project within the main project. Also, when the requirement of the vehicle or equipment is over in a project, it needs to be shifted to another project site where the vehicle or the equipment is required. If the other project does not receive this vehicle or equipment on time, then that project may have to unnecessarily rent or purchase similar vehicle or equipment for the project execution. This adds further to the project cost and reduces overall profitability of the organisation. However, it may be so that after the requirement of a vehicle or equipment in one site is over, there may not be any requirement of that vehicle or equipment immediately in another site. Thus the vehicle or equipment remains idle. Idling cost of vehicle or equipment must also be considered as cost of the project.
    11→
    12→Often managers do not monitor the usage and productivity of vehicles and  
    13→equipment used at site. They simply provide the vehicle and equipment to a project whenever required, get them back when required though often late and left to idling, and shift them to other sites. They do not evaluate the usage of the vehicle or the equipment vis-à-vis the costs incurred as discussed earlier. Not sending vehicle or equipment on time leads to delay in execution of other projects. It so also happens that some “intelligent” Site managers place requisition of vehicle and equipment much earlier than required out of apprehension that he may not get them on time when actually required. In such cases, the vehicle and the equipment lie idle in the project site thereby reducing the utility of the vehicle or the equipment. The FEM Manager must also consider the time at which the project sites really require a particular vehicle or equipment and should not place his requisition in advance.
    14→
    15→Gains or internal profits arising out of usage of equipment must be monitored. Otherwise the purchase or rent of a vehicle or equipment cannot be justified. It’s like purchasing a car but seldom using it. If you require a car for only a day in a month, then better hire a car or take the services of Uber/Ola for the day rather than purchasing the car. But for vehicle or equipment, purchasing or renting is not as easy as purchasing or renting a car. Before purchasing or renting a vehicle or equipment, all Site Managers must agree to use it effectively so that the productivity tagged against each vehicle or equipment is achieved. It is the duty of the Site Manager, the Project Manager and the FEM Manager to ensure productivity of the vehicles and equipment being used at site. Every month, these managers should evaluate the productivity of the vehicles and equipment in use and make corrective actions if required. Vehicles and equipment contribute significantly to the cost of an organization, hence evaluation of their monitoring and optimized usage is essential.
    16→
    17→**The salient points are:**
    18→
    19→1. Inventory of fleet and equipment must be recorded  
    20→2. Cost of fleet and equipment, whether purchased or rented, along with their maintenance costs must be recorded.  
    21→3. Usage of fleet and equipment at each project site must be noted  
    22→4. At the end of each month, utility of fleet and equipment employed at each site vis-à-vis their costs must be evaluated.
    23→

1→---
     2→title: "LACK OF CHECKS AFTER PROJECT COMPLETION"
     3→part: 12
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2660-2676"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : LACK OF CHECKS AFTER PROJECT COMPLETION**
     9→
    10→Every house or a car needs proper maintenance. Similarly it’s true for vehicles and equipment too used at various project sites. Even if a car is running smoothly, it goes for annual maintenance where some inherent non observable defects may be discovered. If such defects are not dealt with on time, the car may break down later. Similar thing may happen to a vehicle or equipment.
    11→
    12→Therefore, every vehicle and equipment must be subjected to annual maintenance as well as regular maintenance as and when necessary. I have seen that some equipment or parts, not being used currently in any of the projects, lie in the equipment store yards for years without maintenance. By the time such equipment is required for a particular site, the equipment is found inoperable or rusted and has to be sold as scrap. Thus the organisation has to purchase or rent similar equipment again. This means adding costs to the company.
    13→
    14→As illustrated before, FEM Managers must not only monitor the use and optimisation of each vehicle and equipment at site but also pay heed to required maintenance of these vehicles and equipment to upkeep their proper health. Often this is not done and the vehicles or equipment not in use idle at storeyards without annual maintenance. In fact I have observed that their storage at the yards is also very poor with no covers to shelter them from polluted atmosphere, rain and dust. 
    15→
    16→Just like we human beings undergo annual health check up to upkeep our bodies in good health, same kind of treatment is also required for vehicles and equipment to extend their healthy lives. 
    17→
    18→**The salient points of concern are:**
    19→
    20→1. Log of regular maintenance of vehicles and equipment as and when found necessary must be maintained  
    21→2. Log of annual maintenance of vehicles and equipment must be maintained.  
    22→3. Excess quantity of vehicles and equipment unlikely to be used in future given the number and type of projects predicted for future may be sold out to arrest further idling and depreciation costs  
    23→4. Annual maintenance costs of vehicles and equipment need to be considered in bid costs
    24→

     7→
     8→**STORES MANAGEMENT**
     9→
    10→**Role of Stores Management in a corporate**  
    11→It oversees the receipt, storage, issuing and distribution of materials as and when required. Inventory management is a key aspect of stores management whereby it has to monitor and keep records of inventory to avoid stockouts and overstocks.
    12→
    13→Stores are the interface between suppliers and site. It receives materials, equipment and machinery from different sources and provides them to sites as and when required. Many materials are supplied in bulk and therefore they cannot be delivered to sites immediately, rather they are stored in the designated stores and sent to sites in batches as per requirement of the project. Sometimes large equipment required in few quantities may be directly dispatched to sites.  
    14→
    15→Stores management deals with (1) checks for quality and quantity of receipts and reject and send back defective items, excess quantity items or list short items which needs to be replenished (2) proper storage of materials (3) dispatching materials to sites (4) maintaining inventory of receipts, dispatch and balance materials at stores.
    16→

1→---
     2→title: "NO RECONCILIATION"
     3→part: 13
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2705-2722"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : NO RECONCILIATION**
     9→
    10→I have observed that when a vendor supplies its materials to the site stores of the organisation, the Stores Managers sometimes blindly sign and accept the quality and quantity of materials without due verification.
    11→
    12→Later on at the end of the project, a Stores Manager may realise shortages in materials and places his request to the management for requisition of materials again. Such requisition may also happen due to some materials being rejected by the client on quality issues.
    13→
    14→Such Site Managers need to be cautioned for their lack in performing their duties and also for increasing cost of the project and leading to delay in the project since the requisitioned materials needs to be processed again through the entire Supply Chain Management process thus requiring substantial time.
    15→
    16→When materials are dispatched to site stores for a project, it is understood that the materials have been technically tested in laboratories. But this does not ensure the correct quantity of materials or damaged materials reaching the stores. Claims forwarded to suppliers for materials shortage or defective are usually rejected by the suppliers till a signed reconciliation is available on receipt of materials at stores. 
    17→
    18→**The salient points :**
    19→
    20→1. On receipt of materials, size and quantity of acceptable and defective materials must be recorded.  
    21→2. The above record must be signed by the Stores Manager and the supplier  
    22→3. Size and quantity of materials being dispatched to site must be recorded with signatures of the Stores Manager and the Site Manager  
    23→4. Excess quantity of materials should be sent back to the supplier at supplier’s cost. Short materials need to be replenished by the supplier at the earliest at supplier’s cost.  
    24→5. Stores Manager must flag diminishing quantity of materials at stores to the Project Manager so that these items, if further required in the project, can be ordered on time.
    25→

     2→title: "IMPROPER STORAGE"
     3→part: 13

     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : IMPROPER STORAGE**
     9→
    10→Storage is an important aspect of Stores management. There are several standards which lay down the procedures of storage for different kind of materials. 
    11→
    12→I have observed steel reinforcement being dumped on open yards in the stores without cover, as a result of which the steel rusts over a period of time. They are sometimes even exposed to rains due to carelessness and ignorance. Using such materials in project can endanger the safety of structures.
    13→
    14→Similarly sometimes cement is stored in the open. With moisture around, sometimes the cement solidifies like a stone and the cement is rendered unusable. 
    15→
    16→**The salient points are:**
    17→
    18→1. Depending on the different types and quantities of materials to be stored, areas for keeping these materials need to be earmarked first.  
    19→2. Based on standard procedures of storage, the materials should be stored.
    20→

     8→**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→**Camera in stores**  
    11→Cameras may be installed at various location of stores for the Project Manager to oversee stores management.
    12→
    13→**Proper storage facilities**  
    14→Different materials need to be stored differently. Some needs open space and some may need sheds and some costly small items may need lockable rooms. 
    15→
    16→**Segregation of damaged materials**  
    17→Damaged supplies received at stores should be segregated separately, photo and video graphed for evidence, and immediately claimed to the supplier for replacement.
    18→

As and when stock of a material diminishes, the Stores Manager must inform the Site Manager and the Project Manager well in time for its replenishment on time.     21→

     2→title: "COMPROMISING QUALITY AGAINST TIME"
     3→part: 14
     4→section: 3

     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : COMPROMISING QUALITY AGAINST TIME**
     9→
    10→Implementation of procedures laid down in the Quality Manual of an organization while executing projects make consume more time than if the measures were not to be taken. 
    11→
    12→For example, during concreting of foundations of a structure, the mixing of coarse aggregates, sand and cement must follow a particular order; the  coarse aggregates need to be put inside the mixer first, then the sand and lastly the cement. Sand tries to occupy the internal spaces within the mass of the coarse aggregates, and cement tries to occupy the internal spaces within the mass of sand; hence this order should be maintained to get a perfectly blended mixture called concrete. 
    13→
    14→But I have often found that this order of mixing the materials for concrete is haywire. No order is maintained. Also the quantity of water put in the mixture is high making the concrete more liquid and easy to mix. All these quality issues result in a bad quality concrete and therefore the structure resting on foundations cast with such bad quality concrete may spell disaster in future.
    15→
    16→I cannot discuss hundreds of Quality checks that needs to be implemented based on the Quality manuals. But I have given a simple example to demonstrate how quality standards are flouted. The issue is when a project is nearing scheduled completion time but delayed, the contractor tries to avoid all these quality checks to speed up the project execution. In the process, the quality of the project is compromised. Many a times, it’s not only for time to be saved, it’s also ignorance and paying no importance to quality.
    17→
    18→**The salient points are:**
    19→
    20→1. Quality is an important aspect of any project.   
    21→2. Quality Manual must be handy and followed by the all departments of an organization.  
    22→3. Quality Head must ensure that proper quality checks are implemented by all the departments. Quality does not only relate to product manufacturing and construction, it also relates to services provided by the departments involved in Designs, Bidding, Supply Chain Management, Fleet Management, etc.
    23→

1→---
     2→title: "BU HEAD BEING MADE RESPONSIBLE"
     3→part: 14
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2789-2803"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : BU HEAD BEING MADE RESPONSIBLE**
     9→
    10→Many Site Managers report to a Project Manager, many Project Managers report to a Project Head and many Project Heads report to a BU Head (Projects) and many BU Heads of different domains report to a SBU Head. There are so many layers of positions between a Site Manager and the BU Head or the SBU Head. It is not possible for a BU Head to track all the ongoing activities at site for which a Site Manager and his boss, the Project Manager is directly responsible. Assuming responsibility further up is asking for too much in my opinion. It is like the President of a very large country being responsible for a small local train accident in a remote village location. 
    11→
    12→So when a quality issue arises at site, why a BU Head or a SBU Head is reprimanded by his Director or the Board? I can extend the illogic further up. I can ask why not the COO be responsible for a lapse in quality since all SBU Heads report to the COO? Why is not the Managing Director responsible since all COOs report to the MD? There has to be a limit to a position to which the buck stops.
    13→
    14→I think the buck should stop at the Project Head level which is below the BU Head level since the Project Head is in-charge of all projects. At most the BU Head could be requested to look into such lapses as to why they have occurred under the supervision of the Project Head. It will be of no use or the Board or MD to reprimand the BU Head or SBU Head because even if reprimanded the BU Head or the SBU Head cannot look into the operations at site all the time. Rather the Project Head has to be interrogated and thereafter systems put in place so that such undesirable incidents do not further occur at site. This commands Project Heads to be positioned at high levels taking responsibility in all matters of project execution.
    15→
    16→If you look at the BU Head’s roles and responsibilities, you will find that he has no time to look into the activities happening at site for which the Site Manager and the Project Manager and the Project Heads are appointed. Therefore the system of rebuking the BU Head or the SBU Head by the Board in such circumstances is meaningless.
    17→
    18→**The salient points are:**
    19→
    20→1. The Project Head must be positioned at a high level taking responsibility of project execution including maintaining Quality standards.  
    21→2. Rebuking BU Head or the SBU Head for quality issues is meaningless
    22→

     8→**CHAPTER : SADISTIC PLEASURE IN REPORTING TO HIGHEST AUTHORITY**

    10→Sometimes, in case of a Quality issue at site, the Quality Manager derives pleasure in reporting the matter to the Quality Head of the organization or to the Managing Director, if he is authorized to do so. The Quality Manager thinks that reporting as many possible quality issues he comes across will prove his magnificent performance which may lead to his promotion. In such cases, the Quality Manager does not act as a facilitator to improving quality standards in a project; rather he becomes a spoilsport creating enmity with his colleagues. 
    11→
    12→The Quality Manager does not understand that he is not only wasting his precious time in pointing out very mundane matters which can be easily sorted out with discussions with the Site/Project Manager but also wasting precious time of the Quality Head or the Managing Director who have much more important work to do.

    14→**The salient points are:**

1→---
     2→title: "COMPROMISING SAFETY AGAINST TIME"
     3→part: 15
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2856-2869"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : COMPROMISING SAFETY AGAINST TIME**
     9→
    10→Implementation of procedures laid down in the Safety Manual of an organization while executing projects make consume more time than if the measures were not to be taken. 
    11→
    12→For example, during outside plastering of painting of a multistoried building, the scaffolding must be checked every day for its safety and all workers must strictly wear their safety belts. But then these checks are often ignores. Many a times courageous work force insists in not wearing safety belts citing that it hampers their work. They are agile and can climb or descend like monkeys. However, there have been cases that a labourer have slipped off the scaffolding and have fallen to death. Under no circumstances can safety be compromised. Human life is very precious.  
    13→
    14→I cannot discuss hundreds of Safety checks that needs to be implemented based on the Safety manuals. But I have mentioned a simple example to demonstrate how safety standards are flouted. The issue is when a project is nearing scheduled completion time but delayed, the contractor tries to avoid all these safety checks to speed up the project execution. In the process, the safety in constructing the project is compromised. Many a times, it’s not only for time to be saved, it’s also ignorance and paying no importance to safety.
    15→
    16→**The salient points are:**
    17→
    18→1. Safety is an important aspect of any project.   
    19→2. Safety Manual must be handy and followed by the all departments of an organization.  
    20→3. Safety Head must ensure that proper quality checks are implemented by all the departments. Safety does not only relate to product manufacturing and construction, it also relates to services provided by the departments involved in Designs, Supply Chain Management, Fleet Management, etc.
    21→

1→---
     2→title: "BU HEAD BEING MADE RESPONSIBLE"
     3→part: 15
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2870-2884"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : BU HEAD BEING MADE RESPONSIBLE**
     9→
    10→Many Site Managers report to a Project Manager, many Project Managers report to a Project Head and many Project Heads report to a BU Head (Projects) and many BU Heads of different domains report to a SBU Head. There are so many layers of positions between a Site Manager and the BU Head or the SBU Head. It is not possible for a BU Head to track all the ongoing activities at site for which a Site Manager and his boss, the Project Manager is directly responsible. Assuming responsibility further up is asking for too much in my opinion. It is like the President of a very large country being responsible for a small local train accident in a remote village location. 
    11→
    12→So when a safety issue arises at site, why a BU Head or a SBU Head is reprimanded by his Director or the Board? I can extend the illogic further up. I can ask why not the COO be responsible for a lapse in quality since all SBU Heads report to the COO? Why is not the Managing Director responsible since all COOs report to the MD? There has to be a limit to a position to which the buck stops.
    13→
    14→I think the buck should stop at the Project Head level which is below the BU Head level since the Project Head is in-charge of all projects. At most the BU Head could be requested to look into such lapses as to why they have occurred under the supervision of the Project Head. It will be of no use or the Board or MD to reprimand the BU Head or SBU Head because even if reprimanded the BU Head or the SBU Head cannot look into the operations at site all the time. Rather the Project Head has to be interrogated and thereafter systems put in place so that such undesirable incidents do not further occur at site. This commands Project Heads to be positioned at high levels taking responsibility in all matters of project execution.
    15→
    16→If you look at the BU Head’s roles and responsibilities, you will find that he has no time to look into the activities happening at site for which the Site Manager and the Project Manager and the Project Heads are appointed. Therefore the system of rebuking the BU Head or the SBU Head by the Board in such circumstances is meaningless.
    17→
    18→**The salient points are:**
    19→
    20→1. The Project Head must be positioned at a high level taking responsibility of project execution including maintaining Quality standards.  
    21→2. Rebuking BU Head or the SBU Head for quality issues is meaningless
    22→

     2→title: "SADISTIC PLEASURE IN REPORTING TO HIGHEST AUTHORITY"
     3→part: 15
     4→section: 3
     5→original_lines: "2885-2896"
     6→---
     7→
     8→**CHAPTER : SADISTIC PLEASURE IN REPORTING TO HIGHEST AUTHORITY**
     9→
    10→Sometimes, in case of a safety issue at site, the Safety Manager derives pleasure in reporting the matter to the Safety Head of the organization or to the Managing Director, if he is authorized to do so. Many unintentional minor lapses can be sorted out between the Safety Manager and the Project Manager or the BU Head, but the Safety Manager does not wish to miss opportunities to report to their higher ups just to gather some brownie points displaying his performance and loyalty. The Safety Manager thinks that reporting as many possible quality issues he comes across will prove his magnificent performance which may lead to his promotion. In such cases, the Safety Manager does not act as a facilitator to improving quality standards in a project; rather he becomes a spoilsport creating enmity with his colleagues. 
    11→
    12→The Safety Manager does not understand that he is not only wasting his precious time in pointing out very mundane matters which can be easily sorted out with discussions with the Site/Project Manager but also wasting precious time of the Safety Head or the Managing Director who have much more important work to do.
    13→
    14→**The salient points are:**
    15→
    16→1. In cases of minor safety issues arising unintentionally, the Safety Manager can discuss with the Site Manager or the Project Manager for a resolution.  
    17→2. Referring minor safety issues every now and then to the Safety Head or the MD serve no purpose for the welfare of the project.  
    18→3. Major safety issues must be highlighted to the top management so that the Project Head is warned and become cautious henceforth and ensure that such issues do not occur in future.
    19→

     7→
     8→**SOME GOOD PRACTICES**
     9→
    10→**Camera**  
    11→Install live cameras with recordings all around the site so that the Project Manager who may be in charge of many sites can oversee the sites from time to time. This will also enable all workers and employees at site to be careful because they know that they are being watched for their work which demands good quality, safety and ethics and utilization of time.,   
    12→
    13→**Sudden Quality inspections**  
    14→Project Head or the BU Head or the Audit (Internal or External) can make sudden visits to site to check on the performance, quality, safety and ethics being practiced at site
    15→
    16→**Top driven**  
    17→To ensure safety standards across the organization, the standards must be top management driven. It is often seen that employees down below ignore safety standards when they observe that it is not top driven.
    18→
    19→**Pay importance to near misses**  
    20→There can be a fatal incidents amongst hundreds of near misses. Do not ignore them. 
    21→
    22→**Identify hazards**  
    23→Identifying harards and hazard zones can save lives of employees and the public. Regular hazard assessments are crucial.

**QUALITY**

**Role of Quality in a corporate**
Quality is crucial in business as it builds customer trust, enhances brand reputation, and drives profitability.

Quality products and services are fundamental to establishing trust with customers. When businesses consistently deliver high-quality offerings, they foster loyalty and confidence among consumers.

A strong reputation for quality can differentiate a business in a competitive market. Companies known for their high-quality products can command higher prices in the market and maintain customer loyalty.

Quality directly impacts customer satisfaction. When products meet or exceed customer expectations, it leads to positive experiences and encourages repeat purchases.

High-quality products often lead to lower costs associated with returns, repairs and replacements.

In a crowded marketplace, quality can be a significant differentiator. Businesses that prioritise quality management and continuous improvement can set industry standards and lead the market.

Quality is a very important component of project execution. Today people have become very conscious of the quality of a product or service. The quality of products is checked in the test laboratories, stores and also at execution sites. At site, the quality of construction deployed has to be of prime importance. A bad quality product or service may not last for long or may lead to defects in the short term.

A cheap item may not necessarily be a bad quality product. However, it must conform to the technical specifications as laid out in the contract documents by the client. The quality required in a product or service is stipulated in the technical specifications of the client. So the contractor has to engage an appropriate supplier or contractor who would be able to deliver the product or service in line with the specifications.

---

part: 15

**SAFETY**

**Role of Safety in a corporate**
The role of safety in corporates and projects is crucial for ensuring the well-being of employees and the successful execution of projects.

Key aspects include:

1) Establishing a safe environment

2) Project Safety Management: Effective safety project management integrates safety principles into the planning, execution, monitoring, and completion of projects, ensuring that all activities prioritise safety.

3) Legal Compliance: Prioritising safety is essential for compliance with regulations and standards, which can prevent legal issues and financial burdens.

4) Leadership and Culture: Leadership must foster a culture where safety is non-negotiable, and all team members are engaged in safety practices.

5) Continuous Improvement: Organisations should continuously learn and improve safety practices, focusing on learning from failures, near misses, and positive work experiences.

By prioritising safety, organisations can enhance productivity, reduce costs, and build trust with stakeholders.

Safety is a very important component of project execution. Today people have become very conscious of the safety of a product or service. The safety of products is checked in the test laboratories, stores and also at execution sites. At site, the safety of construction deployed has to be of prime importance. An unsafe product or service may not last for long or may lead to casualties in the short term.

For example, when a foundation is being cast at a significant depth below the ground level, at the end of the day, the area must be cordoned off with sufficient lighting so that no person falls into the pit. However, this is not done at many sites, endangering lives.
Safety standards of a product must conform to the technical specifications as laid out in the contract documents by the client. So the contractor has to engage an appropriate supplier or contractor who would be able to maintain safety of products and services in line with the specifications.
